rationale_for_baseline_tier: Classic plantar fasciitis with a negative stress-fracture/rupture/neuropathic screen is self-managed with stretching, footwear support, activity modification and analgesia (T0). A stress-fracture safety-net routes to imaging/review (T2); neurovascular compromise/tendon rupture to ED (T4).

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Heel pain becoming a constant dull ache at night/rest, or pain on squeezing the sides of the heel bone
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests a calcaneal stress fracture needing imaging/offloading.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the heel starts aching constantly at night or at rest, or squeezing the sides of the heel bone becomes painful — see your GP; you may need an X-ray.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden inability to push off / rise onto the toes, or the foot becoming numb, cold or pale
escalation_edges.rationale: Possible Achilles rupture or neurovascular compromise.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Emphasise supportive footwear indoors and avoiding barefoot walking.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Stretch your calf and the sole of your foot (including before your first steps), wear supportive cushioned shoes, avoid barefoot on hard floors, and use short-course pain relief. It settles with time — an X-ray isn't needed now.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Self-care + GP/podiatry review in 1–2 weeks; review/imaging if stress-fracture features
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: ED for a numb/cold foot or sudden loss of push-off.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Plantar fasciitis (medial calcaneal); stress-fracture/rupture/neuropathic screens negative; stretching + footwear + analgesia; podiatry if persistent.

critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A calcaneal stress fracture is treated as fasciitis and progresses.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: No stretching/footwear advice, leaving the overload untreated.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: ED/urgent imaging for classic plantar fasciitis — unnecessary.
